narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 61.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, LSAT score, undergraduate GPA, family income bracket, and enrollment status as a full-time student. The three biggest factors shaping this outcome were your Year 3 decile ranking, your undergraduate GPA, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Most significantly, your Year 3 decile of 3—well below the average of about 5.9 for those who passed the bar—strongly pushed the model toward predicting failure, as it signals a dip in performance relative to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which exceeds the 3.24 average among bar passers, worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success. Your LSAT score of 33, however, contributed toward the failure prediction, as it falls short of the typical 37-plus seen in successful students. Compared to admitted applicants, who often have stronger Year 3 rankings and higher LSATs alongside solid GPAs, your profile showed enough risk in those key areas to tip the scales. Your upper-middle family income aligns well with many who pass, and your full-time status matches the norm for 93% of successful students, but these didn't outweigh the top concerns. While this is disappointing, especially with your strong undergrad record, it highlights where the model saw the greatest hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, and reflect on strategies to improve late-stage academic performance, perhaps through targeted tutoring or additional coursework. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience could also help in reapplying to other programs with more holistic reviews. You're clearly capable, as your GPA shows—use this feedback to strengthen those pivotal areas and reapply with confidence.
